---
title: "R Project — data analysis or simulation report"
subtitle: "Your second major portfolio piece"
toc: true
---

> **Pairs with:** the R block of the course (weeks 4–6) and the Week
> 11 R Project conference. Due date and submission link live in
> Blackboard.

## Goal

Build a small, clean R analysis delivered as a **Quarto report**.
You can choose one of two tracks:

- **Track A — Data analysis and visualization.** Tell a short visual
  story from a tidy dataset using `dplyr` and `ggplot2`.
- **Track B — Simulation.** Investigate a simple stochastic question
  by simulation (e.g., a CLT demonstration).

Pick whichever track fits how you like to think.

## What to submit

Submit to Blackboard:

- Your **Quarto source** — `Lastname_First_RProject.qmd`.
- The **rendered PDF** — `Lastname_First_RProject.pdf`.
- Any **external files** the script reads (small CSVs, a `.RData`, etc.)
  in a `data/` subfolder of your submission.
- An **AI Use Note** inside the report — see
  [resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd](../resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd).

Your project must **render top to bottom** with no manual edits, from
a clean folder, by running `quarto render Lastname_First_RProject.qmd`.

## Choose **one** track

### Track A — Data analysis and visualization

Pick **one tidy dataset** and tell a short visual story.

- Aim for **at least 4 plots** using **at least 3 different `geom_*`
  types**, and **at least one grouped summary table** (via
  `dplyr::summarise`).
- Open with the dataset and a research-style question, work through
  4+ figures, and end with a 1–2 paragraph summary of what you found.

**Where the data can come from** — see the full guidance at
[resources/data-guidelines.qmd](../resources/data-guidelines.qmd). The
short version: **built-in R datasets**, **public datasets with
verifiable provenance and license**, or a **dataset you bring
yourself with instructor approval**. Simulated data is welcome for
Track A if your question is amenable.

Examples of good Track A datasets:

- Built-in: `mtcars`, `iris`, `palmerpenguins::penguins`,
  `gapminder::gapminder`, `ggplot2::diamonds`, `dplyr::starwars`.
- Public, well-documented: OpenIntro datasets
  (<https://www.openintro.org/data/>), TidyTuesday archives, official
  statistical agencies.
- Bring-your-own: clear it with me first so we don't run into licensing
  surprises.

### Track B — Simulation and the Normal

Investigate a Normal-based question and compare theoretical and
simulated behavior.

1. **Pick parameters.** Use \(N(\mu, \sigma)\). Either default to
   \((0, 1)\) or estimate \(\hat\mu, \hat\sigma\) from a numeric
   variable in a public dataset.
2. **Overlay theoretical and simulated distributions.** Draw a large
   sample (e.g., \(n = 1000\)) from \(N(\mu, \sigma)\). Plot a
   histogram or density; overlay the theoretical Normal curve.
3. **Sampling distribution of the mean (CLT demo).** Repeat sampling
   (500 or 1000 replicates of size \(m\)) to get sample means. Plot
   the distribution of means; compare its mean and SD to \(\mu\) and
   \(\sigma / \sqrt{m}\).
4. **Reflect.** A short paragraph explaining what the simulation
   confirms (or doesn't).

For Track B, **set a seed** at the top of your document
(`set.seed(...)`) so your numbers reproduce.

## Required deliverables (both tracks)

1. **Reproducible source.** Top-to-bottom rendering. Seeds set where
   simulation is used. Relative paths for any external files.
2. **Figure quality.** Every plot has a **title**, **axis labels with
   units**, **legible text**, **no overlapping labels**, and a
   sensible theme (e.g., `theme_minimal()`).
3. **Narration.** Each figure has a 1–3 sentence interpretation
   immediately around it.
4. **Code quality.** Clear section headers, idiomatic tidyverse where
   appropriate, no commented-out experiments dangling at the end.
5. **AI Use Note** at the end of the report.

## Grading

This project is evaluated across the course's four provisional
categories, with most of the weight in **R** and **technical
workflow**, smaller portions on **mathematical writing** (figure
captions, prose) and **generative AI literacy** (the AI Use Note).

Specific category weights for the term are announced in Blackboard.

## AI use

You may use AI to help with R syntax, `ggplot2` polish, or debugging.
Include an **AI Use Note** in your report with the three labeled
lines: **Tool**, **Purpose**, **Verification**. See
[resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd](../resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd).

Pitfalls to watch for:

- AI suggesting **packages that aren't installed** or that don't exist.
- AI rewriting your code in a way you don't understand — don't accept
  this. Ask it to explain the change first.
- AI generating plot code that looks fine but **silently drops rows**
  (e.g., changing `na.rm` behavior). Sanity-check `nrow()` before and
  after wrangling.

## Data note

This course **does not use legacy archive datasets from prior terms**.
All data you use should come from the categories listed at
[resources/data-guidelines.qmd](../resources/data-guidelines.qmd):
built-in R, simulated, public-with-license, or instructor-approved
student-selected.
